name: The faithful and wise servant
notes: A twofold synoptic parable from the Olivet teaching, given in two complementary settings: Matthew sets it within the discourse on the mount of Olives, Luke within the journey teaching prompted by Peter's question. The shared core is two figures: the watching householder who would have guarded his house had he known the thief's hour, and the steward set over the household, faithful and wise if found feeding them in due season, but evil if he presumes 'My lord delayeth his coming' and turns to smiting and drunkenness. Both close with the lord coming in an unlooked-for hour and cutting the unfaithful servant asunder, appointing his portion with the hypocrites (Matthew) or unbelievers (Luke). Matthew speaks of 'meat,' 'his goods,' and adds the weeping and gnashing of teeth; Luke speaks of 'their portion of meat,' 'all that he hath,' the menservants and maidens, and frames the saying as answering Peter's question. These differing emphases are complementary vantages on one parable.

be ye also ready: for in such an hour as ye think not the Son of man cometh
Who then is a faithful and wise servant ... whom his lord hath made ruler over his household, to give them meat in due season
Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing
he shall make him ruler over all
if that evil servant shall say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming
shall begin to smite ... and to eat and drink with the drunken
shall come in a day when he looketh not for him, and in an hour that he is not aware of
cut him asunder, and appoint him his portion with the hypocrites
